seoTitle: How to Record a Vehicle Purchase in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Learn how to properly book a business vehicle purchase as a fixed asset and loan liability in QuickBooks Online, not an expense.
intro: Buying a company truck or car is a capital expenditure, not a simple expense, and recording it incorrectly can distort your Profit and Loss statement and mess up depreciation later. This guide walks through setting up the right fixed asset and loan accounts, then using a journal entry to record the purchase correctly. It's for business owners handling their first vehicle acquisition in QuickBooks.

steps.heading: Create the necessary ledger accounts
steps.body: In Chart of Accounts, set up a Fixed Asset account for the vehicle and a Long-Term Liability account for the auto loan if they don't already exist.

steps.heading: Open a journal entry
steps.body: Click + New, then choose Journal Entry under the Other column to record the multi-part transaction.

steps.heading: Record the vehicle's total cost
steps.body: In the first row, select the new vehicle fixed asset account and enter the full purchase price under Debits.

steps.heading: Map the loan and any cash down payment
steps.body: Add a row for the auto loan liability account with the financed amount under Credits, and a row for your checking account if a cash down payment was made.

steps.heading: Add fees and save
steps.body: Include any non-financed dealer fees to keep the entry balanced, then save and run a fresh Balance Sheet to confirm the asset and loan appear correctly.

faq.question: Why shouldn't I just record the vehicle as an expense?
faq.answer: Mapping a vehicle purchase to an expense account overstates your costs for that period and skips proper asset tracking needed for depreciation.

faq.question: How do I handle a trade-in on the old vehicle?
faq.answer: A trade-in should be recorded as a reduction to the purchase price or its own line in the journal entry, since it affects the total financed amount.

faq.question: Does this journal entry account for depreciation?
faq.answer: No, depreciation is a separate, ongoing entry your accountant will typically set up based on the asset's useful life.

faq.question: What if my journal entry doesn't balance?
faq.answer: Double-check that your debits and credits total the same amount, including any fees or down payment rows you added.
